title: Replacing a light switch
subtitle: Isolate, prove dead, transfer conductors, test
excerpt: How to replace a single-gang one-way or two-way light switch faceplate: isolating the lighting circuit, proving dead with the live-dead-live procedure, transferring conductors to the new switch, and testing before restoring the supply.
sourceNotes: Standard UK domestic electrical practice per IET Wiring Regulations 18th Edition (BS 7671). Two-way switching cable colours from the 2006 harmonised colours amendment. Safe isolation procedure from HSE guidance.

content.text: Swapping a switch faceplate follows the same safe-isolation steps as swapping a socket faceplate. Isolate the circuit, lock it off, prove it dead with a live-dead-live test, photograph the connections, transfer them, refit, restore the supply, and test the switch. The extra step is at a two-way switch. A two-way switch has three terminals and a three-core-and-earth cable, not a simple twin-and-earth.
Safe isolation
Step 1, identify and isolate the circuit.

Switch on the light to confirm it works, then go to the consumer unit and switch off the lighting circuit MCB. Apply the lock-off device. Return to the switch and check the light no longer works. Perform the full live-dead-live test with the two-pole tester on the switch terminals after removing the faceplate as described in the socket replacement article.
Removing the old switch
Step 2, unscrew and remove the faceplate.
Undo the two retaining screws and pull the faceplate away from the wall box. Photograph the wiring before touching it.
One-way switch wiring.

A one-way switch has two terminals. Twin-and-earth cable connects brown (line in) to one terminal and grey or black sleeved in red/brown (switch wire) to the other. The bare earth is sleeved green-and-yellow at the earth terminal on the switch plate.
Two-way switch wiring.
A two-way switch has three terminals: Com (common), L1, and L2.
Three-core-and-earth

cable connects the brown conductor to Com, grey to L1, and black (both sleeved in the harmonised colours) to L2. Pre-2006 installations use red (L1), yellow (L2), and blue (Com). The photograph rule is especially important here.
Fitting the new switch
Step 3, connect the new faceplate.
Transfer each conductor to the matching terminal on the new switch, following the photograph. Use screw-terminal fixings only; backstab push-in terminals are not suitable for lighting circuits.
Step 4, fold and fix.
Fold the cables into the wall box carefully and fix the faceplate. The faceplate should sit flush; tighten the retaining screws evenly.
Testing
Step 5, restore and test.
Remove the lock-off device and restore the circuit. Toggle the switch and confirm the light operates. For a two-way circuit, test from both switch positions.
Common mistakes
Light stays on when the switch is in the off position.
The switch wire and the line feed are swapped at the one-way terminals. Switch off, re-isolate, swap the conductors, and retest.
Two-way circuit: light on from one switch, off position does not work from the other.
L1 and L2 are swapped. Switch off, re-isolate, swap the conductors at L1 and L2, and retest.
